skill.name: verification-quality-assurance
skill.description: Verification and quality assurance guidance for Claude Flow workflows, including truth scoring, checks, reporting, rollback, dashboards, and CI integration.
skill.summary: Verify Claude Flow outputs with truth scoring, reports, rollback workflows, and CI quality checks.
skill.author: ruvnet

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly detected many command, network, filesystem, and environment-access patterns, but they appear in Markdown examples rather than hidden executable code. The skill is not malicious, but it deserves a medium warning because copied workflows can execute external packages, modify Git history, write temporary files, and send metrics or API keys to external services.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed Package and Shell Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly documents shell commands that run npx claude-flow@alpha and CI shell steps. These examples are legitimate for the tool, but users who copy them will execute external package code and local project commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution pattern is real and extensive, but it is presented as user-facing documentation rather than hidden code. Legitimate use is plausible, so the risk is operational rather than clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: Rollback Commands Can Modify Repository State
medium_findings.description: The rollback workflow includes commands that revert files and a troubleshooting example using git reset --hard HEAD~1. This supports the skill purpose, but it can destroy uncommitted work if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive repository operation is explicitly documented and semantically matches rollback behavior. It is not hidden or deceptive, which limits severity.

medium_findings.title: External Metrics Export May Expose Data or API Keys
medium_findings.description: Monitoring examples post verification metrics to external endpoints and include a DataDog API key in a URL query parameter. This can expose quality data or leak keys through shell history, logs, or proxy records.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoints and environment variable expansion are directly shown in the same pipeline. They are examples, but copied usage could disclose sensitive project metrics or API keys.

low_findings.title: Temporary Verification Report in Shared Directory
low_findings.description: The pre-commit hook example writes verification output to /tmp/verify.json. This may expose report details to local processes or collide with another process on shared systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem write is explicit, but the content appears to be a verification report rather than credentials. Impact depends on local system permissions and report sensitivity.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static alerts for weak cryptography and reconnaissance map to metadata, documentation text, exit-code explanations, and ordinary git status troubleshooting. No evidence found of cryptographic operations or reconnaissance intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered context shows descriptive Markdown and standard troubleshooting commands. There is no code path that implements weak cryptography or scans networks.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple examples execute npx claude-flow@alpha commands. Users should review package provenance and run commands only in trusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is a real external command execution path, but it is expected for this CLI-oriented skill and not automatically executed by the skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution in CI Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: CI examples use command substitution to read scores and compare thresholds. This is normal shell syntax but can be fragile if reused with untrusted data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is visible and appears constrained to local verification output. The risk is mainly from users adapting the snippets to untrusted inputs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Posting with Environment-Based API Key
dangerous_patterns.description: The DataDog example posts data to an external API and expands DD_API_KEY in the URL. Header-based authentication would reduce logging exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external URL and API key expansion are directly present. The finding is limited because this is documentation, not autonomous exfiltration code.

content.user_title: Verify Code Quality with Truth Scoring
content.value_statement: Agent-generated code can be difficult to validate consistently. This skill provides Claude Flow verification workflows for truth scores, reports, rollback decisions, and CI checks.
actual_capabilities: Explains Claude Flow truth score commands and score thresholds.
Shows verification checks for files, tasks, directories, and batches.
Documents rollback workflows for failed verification results.
Provides CI examples for GitHub Actions and GitLab pipelines.
Covers dashboard, report, watch, and monitoring export workflows.
limitations: Requires Claude Flow and a compatible Node.js environment.
Does not implement its own verification engine inside the skill file.
Rollback examples can modify repository state if users run them.
External monitoring examples require careful secret handling.

use_cases.title: Review Agent Output Before Merge
use_cases.description: Use verification checks and truth score thresholds before accepting generated code into a branch.

use_cases.title: Add Quality Gates to CI
use_cases.description: Use the documented JSON output and score checks to fail builds below a chosen threshold.

use_cases.title: Track Quality Trends Over Time
use_cases.description: Use report and dashboard workflows to monitor verification scores across agents, tasks, and time periods.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Verification Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for one file or directory.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the verification-quality-assurance skill to check my current project. Show the safest command sequence and explain the expected pass or fail signals.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Truth Score Threshold
prompt_templates.scenario: Set practical quality gates for a project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose verification thresholds for development, staging, and production. Explain the tradeoffs for each threshold.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CI Quality Gate
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a pipeline check from verification results.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a CI quality gate. Include score checks, report artifacts, and failure handling, but avoid exposing secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Safe Rollback Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe verification and rollback plan for a repository. Include dry-run steps, backup checks, and conditions for manual approval.

output_examples.input: Verify a pull request and decide whether it can merge.
output_examples.output: A concise quality review with an overall score, failed criteria, affected files, and a merge recommendation.

output_examples.input: Create a CI quality gate for generated code.
output_examples.output: A pipeline plan that runs verification, stores a report artifact, checks the score threshold, and blocks weak results.

output_examples.input: Investigate a low truth score after agent edits.
output_examples.output: A triage summary that identifies likely failure areas, suggests focused checks, and recommends whether rollback is appropriate.

best_practices: Run verification in dry-run or report mode before enabling rollback.
Use stricter thresholds for production and lower thresholds for experiments.
Store reports as CI artifacts and avoid putting API keys in URLs.
anti_patterns: Do not run rollback commands without checking uncommitted work first.
Do not send verification metrics to external services without reviewing their contents.
Do not treat a truth score as a complete replacement for human review.

faq.question: What does this skill help verify?
faq.answer: It guides verification of code quality, task outputs, truth scores, reports, and rollback decisions in Claude Flow workflows.

faq.question: Does the skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents command examples that users or agents may choose to run in a prepared environment.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The examples execute external commands, modify repositories, write temporary files, and post metrics to external endpoints.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, with workflows centered on Claude Flow commands.

faq.question: What should users review before enabling rollback?
faq.answer: Users should confirm backups, inspect Git status, run dry-run commands, and define clear approval rules.

faq.question: Does it replace tests or security review?
faq.answer: No. It adds quality signals and workflow guidance, but tests and security review remain necessary.
